summary: Monstrous Mountains‑Moon > Dark Star > Saint Stephen > The Eleven stretch
blurb: The Ark show delivers a blistering Mountains Of The Moon → Dark Star → Saint Stephen → The Eleven sequence and a soaring 30‑minute Dark Star. Jerry’s amp‑cranked tone and the twin‑drum precision drive the improvisations, while Tom Constanten’s organ adds depth on the first set.
review: April 22, 1969 at Boston’s Ark captured one of the Dead’s most electrifying late‑60s outings. The first set kicks off with solid roots material and a powerful reading of “Death Don't Have No Mercy,” but it’s the second set that cements this night in legend – a seamless, high‑energy trek through “Dupree's Diamond Blues” into a monster “Mountains Of The Moon → Dark Star → Saint Stephen → The Eleven” suite, capped by a soulful “Turn On Your Lovelight.” Jerry Garcia’s tone is described as blistering and amp‑cranked, especially on the extended Dark Star, while Phil Lesh provides a “monster” bass foundation that lifts The Eleven. Tom Constanten’s organ sits prominently in the mix, adding depth to the early jams, and the twin drummers, Mickey Hart and Bill Kreutzmann, earn high marks for powerful rolls around the 24‑25 minute mark and tight stereo interplay throughout the improvisations. Despite some tape glitches and a flub at the end of “Turn On Your Lovelight,” the show’s intensity and improvisational peaks make it a must‑listen snapshot of the band in peak form.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Both recordings are uniformly positive, praising the monumental Mountains Of The Moon → Dark Star sequence, monster bass, blistering guitar tone, and tight drumming. Multiple sources highlight the same standout moments, indicating strong consensus of a high‑quality performance.

best_recording.reason: Complete capture of the show with usable mix despite minor tape issues; designated as the community’s preferred source.

key_highlights: Monstrous Mountains Of The Moon → Dark Star → Saint Stephen → The Eleven sequence
30‑minute soaring Dark Star in the second set
Powerful drum rolls around the 24‑25 minute mark driving the improvisations
Tom Constanten’s organ adds depth and is well‑balanced in the mix

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia’s tone is blistering and amp‑cranked, delivering massive feedback on Dark Star and soaring solos throughout the set.
band_performance.Phil: Lesh provides a monster bass foundation, especially powerful on The Eleven and underpinning the extended jams.
band_performance.Bob: Weir sits low in the mix during The Eleven but locks into the pocket, contributing solid rhythm when the band builds tension.
band_performance.Mickey: Hart’s drumming features powerful rolls around 24‑25 min, praised for precision and drive.
band_performance.Billy: Kreutzmann’s playing interlocks tightly with Hart, delivering tight stereo placement and propelling the extended improvisations.
